coredata.dc:title: Direct synthesis of A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+, Y3+) solid solutions by ball milling constituent oxides
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2004 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Moreno, Karla J.
Rodrigo, R. Silva
Fuentes, Antonio F.
coredata.dc:description: Different compositions in two solid solutions, A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+, Y3+), with high oxygen ion conductivity, have been successfully prepared at room temperature via mechano-chemical synthesis. Stoichiometric mixtures of the constituent oxides were milled in a planetary ball mill by using zirconia vials and balls. Chemical changes in the powder mixtures as a function of composition and milling time were followed by using X-ray diffraction showing that in all cases and after milling for 19h, the powders consisted of a single phase. Powders were also examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) finding out that they basically consist of sub-micron size agglomerates and aggregates of nanoparticles.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Disordered systems
Mechano-chemical synthesis
X-ray diffraction
Ionic conduction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray powder diffraction patterns showing the evolution of the Y2O3 +TiO2 mixture with milling time (a) and that of the starting powders (b). CY stands for cubic Y2O3; A for anatase TiO2; and YT for Y2Ti2O7.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns showing the evolution of the Y2O3 +ZrO2 mixture with milling time (a) and that of the starting powders (b). CY stands for cubic Y2O3; YSZ for Y2O3 stabilized ZrO2; and YZ for Y2Zr2O7.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns showing the evolution of the Y2O3 +0.9ZrO2 +1.1TiO2 mixture with milling time (a) and that of the starting powders (b). CY stands for cubic Y2O3; YSZ for Y2O3 stabilized ZrO2; A for anatase TiO2; and YZT for Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7.

(a) X-ray powder diffraction patterns showing a structural change with the zirconium content within the Y2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 system, from an ordered pyrochlore for Y2Ti2O7, a disordered pyrochlore Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7 to a defect fluorite ZrO2 (33mol% Y2O3) (from top to bottom). Reflections labeled are some of those characterizing the pyrochlore superstructure their intensity decreasing as the zirconium content increases; (b) X-ray diffraction patterns of the just-milled powders of five compositions prepared with different Ti4+/Zr4+ ratio confirming the formation of the Y2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 solid solution (from top to bottom y =0, 0.35, 0.65, 0.9, 1).

X-ray powder diffraction patterns of the evolution of the Gd2O3 +TiO2 mixture with milling time (a) and that of the starting powders (b). CG stands for cubic Gd2O3; A for anatase TiO2; and GT for Gd2Ti2O7.

X-ray powder diffraction patterns of the evolution with milling time of the Gd2O3 +ZrO2 mixture (a) and the Gd2O3 +0.9ZrO2 +1.1TiO2 mixture (b). GZ stands for Gd2Zr2O7; GZT for Gd2(Ti(0.55)Zr(0.45)); (c) X-ray diffraction patterns of the just-milled powders of five compositions prepared with different Ti4+/Zr4+ ratio confirming the formation of the Gd2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 solid solution (from top to bottom y =0, 0.35, 0.45, 0.9, 1).

Scanning electron microstructures of the agglomerates of sub-micron size of the sample Gd2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7 (a) and the sample Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7 showing that the agglomerates are formed by smaller particles (b).

article.ce:title: Direct synthesis of A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+, Y3+) solid solutions by ball milling constituent oxides

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CINVESTAV-IPN Unidad Saltillo, Carretera Saltillo-Monterrey Km 13, Apartado Postal 663, 25000 Saltillo, Coahuila, México

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: División de Estudios de Postgrado e Investigación del Instituto Tecnológico de Ciudad Madero (ITCM), 89440-Ciudad Madero, Tamaulipas, México

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Different compositions in two solid solutions, A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+, Y3+), with high oxygen ion conductivity, have been successfully prepared at room temperature via mechano-chemical synthesis. Stoichiometric mixtures of the constituent oxides were milled in a planetary ball mill by using zirconia vials and balls. Chemical changes in the powder mixtures as a function of composition and milling time were followed by using X-ray diffraction showing that in all cases and after milling for 19h, the powders consisted of a single phase. Powders were also examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) finding out that they basically consist of sub-micron size agglomerates and aggregates of nanoparticles.

ce:section.ce:para:
Oxides A2B2O7, with a pyrochlore type of structure, are well known ionic conductors with some of them showing at high temperatures, conductivities of the same order of magnitude than that of yttria stabilized zirconia (YSZ) [1]. The fully ordered pyrochlore presents cubic symmetry (S.G.:
F d
3 ¯
m
) and can be described in terms of a superstructure of the ideal defect fluorite structure (cubic, S.G.:
F m
3 ¯
m
) with twice the cell constant, a ≈10Å [2]. Oxygen mobility in these compounds is closely related to structural disorder and while fully ordered pyrochlores such as Gd2Ti2O7, are poor ionic conductors, disordered oxides such as Gd2Zr2O7, present high oxygen conductivity. Interesting enough, different degrees of disorder can be reached in systems of solid solutions by using the appropriate substitutions on the A and B sites. Thus, increasing disorder is obtained as the ratio of the average radii of cations A and B (R A/R B) decreases, leading when the ratio approaches unity (complete disorder), to phases with a defect fluorite type of structure [3]. In this work, we have focused our attention to the mechano-chemical synthesis of two solid solutions A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+ and Y3+), with a pyrochlore type of structure and with potential application in many areas of technological interest because of their high oxygen conductivities [4,5]. Since preparing them by traditional solid state reactions requires prolonged firing at temperatures well above 1000°C, they are normally prepared by the Pechini ‘liquid mix’ technique [6]. This chemical method starts with the dissolution of metal compounds in a mixture of citric acid and ethylene glycol and implies several heating steps to promote polymerization of metal citrates, eliminate organic constituents and obtain the final products, which is normally achieved at temperatures between 700 and 800°C. In this paper, we report a direct method for preparing them at room temperature by ball milling constituent oxides.

Several compositions of general formula A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+ and Y3+; 0≤ y ≤ 1) were prepared by mechano-chemical synthesis. Stoichiometric mixtures of Y2O3 (Aldrich Chem. Inc. 99.99%), TiO2 (Merck 99.9%), ZrO2 (8% Y2O3, Aldrich Chem. Inc. 99.9%), ZrO2 (Aldrich Chem. Inc. 99.99%) and Gd2O3 (Aldrich Chem. Inc. 99.99%) were weighed and placed in 125mL zirconia containers with six 20mm diameter zirconia balls (mass≈24g) as to keep balls to powder mass ratio equal to 10:1. Mechanical milling was performed in air, at room temperature, in a Retsch PM/400 planetary ball mill fitted with two milling stations and by using a rotating disc speed of 350rpm. Three compositions on each solid solution were selected to study the evolution of powder mixtures with composition and milling time, characterizing them by using X-ray powder diffraction in a Philips X’pert diffractometer using Ni-filtered Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5418Å). Ball-milled powders were also examined by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) in a Philips XL30 ESEM microscope equipped with an EDAX Inc. energy-dispersive X-ray detector for microanalysis. Specific surface area (SSA) of the samples was measured by a nitrogen gas adsorption instrument (Quantachrome Autosorb-1) based on the BET method.

For reasons of clarity, results obtained for the two solid solutions studied in this work will be presented separately. In order to follow changes with milling time and chemical composition, three samples were selected as representatives in each system and those would be the results shown here: the two limits, A2Ti2O7 and A2Zr2O7, plus one, A2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7, containing both, Ti4+ and Zr4+.

Fig. 1(a) shows an XRD study of the evolution of the (Y2O3 +2TiO2) mixture with milling time. As a reference, Fig. 1(b) shows the XRD pattern of the starting powder where the main reflections of cubic Y2O3 and anatase TiO2, are labeled. A significant decrease in intensity and broadening of the characteristic reflections of both oxides are observed after the first hour as a consequence of a considerable decrease in particle size and the introduction of a large number of crystal defects. Thus, the intensity of the {222} reflection of cubic Y2O3 is approximately 40% of that of the same line in the starting powder while that of the {101} line of anatase is around 30% of the same line in the initial powder. Nevertheless, their presence is still evident after 3h milling. A phase transformation from cubic to a high-pressure monoclinic form of yttrium oxide, β-Y2O3, has been achieved by milling the first one in a high-energy planetary ball mill using steel vials and steel balls [7]. Although complete transformation is not observed in our case, both polymorphs seem to coexist after the first hour as evidenced by the presence of a group of weak reflections in a region, between 28° and 33° (2θ), where the characteristic peaks of the monoclinic form are found [8]. High-energy ball milling is also known to induce the polymorphic transformation of anatase TiO2, first, into a transient high-pressure orthorhombic form having the α-PbO2 structure, TiO2-II, and with increasing milling time, into tetragonal rutile [9,10]. However, the existence of such transformations cannot be observed with the experimental settings chosen in this work. The presence of this high-pressure form, TiO2-II, cannot be confirmed since its main reflection, the {111} line is found at 2θ =31.490° in an area where the main reflections of monoclinic Y2O3 and even those of the Y2Ti2O7 pyrochlore, are also found. Neither the presence of rutile TiO2 could be established. None of its three most intense reflections, the {110} line at 2θ =27.469°, the {211} line at 2θ =54.370° and the {101} line at 2θ =36.116°, could be observed. The {101} line of anatase is still present after 1 and 3h milling. After 9h, the powder mixture shows an X-ray diffraction pattern where the main reflections of the cubic pyrochlore, (e.g. {222} at 2θ =30.677°), are evident. As Fig. 1(a) shows, further milling produces a significant increase in their intensities. Growth of the crystallites initially formed of this phase or the increment in their number with milling time, could be responsible for this effect. No additional phases seem to be present in the powder.

Fig. 2(a) shows the evolution of the (Y2O3 +2ZrO2) powder mixture with milling time. As a reference, Fig. 2(b) shows the XRD pattern of the initial mixture where the main reflections of cubic Y2O3 and tetragonal YSZ, are clearly seen. It is well known that the equilibrium form of ZrO2 at room temperature presents monoclinic symmetry transforming into tetragonal and cubic forms at temperatures around 1170 and 2370°C, respectively. However, these forms of higher symmetry can also be stabilized at room temperature by the addition of lower valent metal oxides such as CaO, MgO, Y2O3 or rare earth sesquioxides, Ln2O3. High-energy ball milling allows stabilized cubic zirconias to be prepared at room temperature by milling monoclinic ZrO2 and the corresponding oxides [11]. Additionally, complete transformation to tetragonal ZrO2 have been also achieved by this technique without the necessity of using any additives, just by milling pure monoclinic ZrO2 for 1 day in a planetary ball mill [7]. As Fig. 2(a) shows, in our case and with prolonged milling, the characteristic reflections of both chemicals, Y2O3 and YSZ, seem to converge into one set (e.g. the Y2O3 {222} line at 2θ =29.174° and the YSZ {101} line at 2θ =30.196°) which resembles that typical of a fluorite-like structure, ZrO2 (33mol%Y2O3). After milling 19h, these are the only reflections present in the powder diffraction pattern.

Fig. 3(a and b) show respectively, the starting powder pattern and the evolution of the (Y2O3 +0.9ZrO2 +1.1TiO2) mixture with milling time. Powder patterns are very similar to those presented in Fig. 2 but for the presence of the main reflection of anatase TiO2 which disappears after 3h milling. As in the previous mixture, reflections of cubic Y2O3 and tetragonal YSZ converge into a set, which could correspond either to a fluorite or to a pyrochlore-type of material. It is important to remember at this point, that the pyrochlore structure can be considered as a superstructure of an anion deficient fluorite-like atomic arrangement with twice the cell constant. Therefore, its diffraction pattern contains a set of strong intensities characteristic of the underlying fluorite-type average structure plus and additional set of superstructure reflections with intensities depending on factors such as the degree of ordering, difference in average scattering factors of the elements involved, distribution of oxygen vacancies, etc. By using neutron diffraction analysis, Heremans et al. [3] found out that increasing Zr4+ content in the solid solution Y2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7, induces disorder in both, the cation and anion sublattices of the Y2Ti2O7 ordered pyrochlore so that disordered pyrochlores (0.3≤ y ≤0.9) and deflect fluorites (y ≥0.9) are obtained. Accordingly, the characteristic superstructure reflections of pyrochlores present decreasing intensity as the Zr4+ content increases. The composition Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7, should present an X-ray powder diffraction pattern typical of a disordered pyrochlore although such point can not be confirmed in the just milled powder. Therefore, the three compositions selected, Y2Ti2O7, Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7 and ZrO2 (33mol%Y2O3) were fired 12h at 1500°C and analyzed by XRD. As Fig. 4(a) shows, typical patterns of an ordered, pyrochlore, a disordered pyrochlore (Y2(Ti0.55Zr0.45)2O7) and a defect fluorite (ZrO2 (33mol% Y2O3)) were obtained. To support the idea of a solid solution formation on milling, Fig. 4(b) shows a comparison between the XRD patterns of five compositions with different Ti4+/Zr4+ ratio (increasing from top to bottom), as taken out of the planetary ball mill. Although the {331} line distinctive of pyrochlores, is only evident in pure Y2Ti2O7, there is a marked shift on the position of all reflections observed towards low angles and therefore, increase in cell size, as the zirconium content increases, obvious consequence of the difference in ionic radii between Ti4+and Zr4+ (0.605Å versus 0.720Å, both in octahedral coordination) [12].

Fig. 5(a) shows the evolution of the XRD pattern of the (Gd2O3 +2TiO2) mixture with milling time. As a reference, Fig. 5(b) shows the XRD pattern of the initial mixture where the main reflections of cubic Gd2O3 and tetragonal anatase, are clearly seen. As before, characteristic reflections of both chemicals suffered a considerable decrease in intensity (∼80%) and increase in peak broadening during the initial stages of the milling process. Additionally, it is also obvious that ball milling induces a Gd2O3 polymorphic transformation, from the highly symmetric cubic form present in the initial mixture, the so-called C-form in lanthanide sesquioxides, to the monoclinic B-form as evidenced by the formation of new reflections in the 28°–33° region (2θ). This transformation which is normally temperature driven taking place when cubic Gd2O3 is fired at temperatures above 1200°C, could be also caused by the application of very high pressures or high-energy milling [7]. While characteristic reflections of both, B- and C-forms, are evident in the XRD pattern of the powder milled for 1h, monoclinic Gd2O3 seems to be the predominant form when milling time increases to 3h. The powder milled for 9h shows an X-ray diffraction pattern typical of a mostly amorphous product although the main reflections of cubic Gd2Ti2O7, the {222} (2θ =30.403°), {440} (2θ =50.688°) and {622} (2θ =60.252°) lines, are just evident. With prolonged milling (19h), the number and intensity of the characteristic reflections of a pyrochlore-type of phase, present in the powder pattern increases. None of the characteristic reflections of the starting oxides or monoclinic Gd2O3, are apparent in this X-ray powder diffraction pattern suggesting that the chemical reaction is complete.

Fig. 6(a) shows the evolution of the (Gd2O3 +2ZrO2) mixture with milling time. Identification of the chemical species present in this mixture after the initial hour milling period is difficult because of overlapping reflections. Nevertheless, monoclinic ZrO2 and cubic and monoclinic Gd2O3 were identified in the X-ray diffraction pattern. Formation of the pyrochlore Gd2Zr2O7 is already evident after milling for 9h with its main reflections increasing their intensity as milling time increases. The extra reflection observed at 32.67° (2θ) in the powder milled for 9h could not be assigned.

Fig. 6(b) shows the evolution of the (Gd2O3 +0.9ZrO2 +1.1TiO2) mixture with milling time. The evolution of this mixture is similar to that described before with the formation of a pyrochlore type of material being evident after 9h milling. Gadolinium zirconate is a disordered pyrochlore and to transform it into a defect fluorite requires firing at temperatures above 1550°C. Therefore, this solid solution does not present the phase transformation described before when discussing the yttrium containing system since both, Gd2Ti2O7 and Gd2Zr2O7 are pyrochlores. Just as in the yttrium system however, disorder in the anion sublattice increases as the zirconium content increases and one should expect the superlattice reflections decrease their intensity in the same direction.

The above-mentioned observations suggest that the mechanically activated chemical reaction between Gd2O3, TiO2 and ZrO2 involves an initial step of particle size reduction of the constituent oxides together with the introduction of a large number of crystal defects and the polymorphic transformation of, at least, Gd2O3, from that of cubic symmetry to the less symmetric monoclinic form. Formation of Gd2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 pyrochlores seems to take place as a final step starting from an amorphous matrix of these chemicals.

Ionic conductors with a pyrochlore type of structure and chemical formula A2(Ti(1− y)Zr y )2O7 (A=Gd3+, Y3+) have been synthesized at room temperature just by milling stoichiometric mixtures of the constituent oxides in a planetary ball mill. Chemical changes in the powder mixtures were followed by XRD diffraction showing that after 9h milling, there is formation of solid solutions in both systems. Further milling brings about an increase in the intensity of the characteristic reflections of the corresponding products suggesting also that the number of their crystal nuclei formed increases with prolonged milling. This synthesis involves polymorphic transformations from the highly symmetric cubic A2O3 (A=Y, Gd) oxides to the monoclinic forms. As obtained powders were found to consist basically, of sub-micronic agglomerates.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Eur. J. Solid State Inorg. Chem.
